fund.name: ICICI Prudential Large Cap Fund (erstwhile Bluechip Fund) - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Core equity holding — large-cap stocks of India's biggest, most stable companies. Equity-taxed (LTCG 12.5% above ₹1.25L) under new regime. Lower portfolio drag vs active large-cap funds. Will form the wealth-building engine over 30 years. ₹30,000 lumpsum from existing corpus used to kickstart this core holding. SIP of ₹4,800/month.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Passive mid-cap index fund — tracks Nifty Midcap 150, giving exposure to India's mid-sized companies at just 0.20% yearly fee. Equity-taxed. Adds growth tilt without active fund manager risk. ₹2,400/month SIP. No lumpsum recommended here — mid-cap can be volatile in short timeframes.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Aggressive Hybrid fund — holds ~65-80% stocks + 20-35% bonds. Equity-taxed (LTCG 12.5% above ₹1.25L). Acts as the stability anchor for this moderate-risk profile. Lower volatility than pure equity Nifty 50 funds. ₹20,000 lumpsum from existing corpus + ₹800/month SIP. Important note: hybrid funds can be tax-inefficient for new regime investors (slab-taxed on gains). This allocation is kept deliberate and small (10%) to balance risk, not for tax efficiency.

plan.setup_phase: Month 1: Open direct plan accounts on MF Central or your MF provider's direct platform. Deploy ₹30,000 lumpsum into the Large Cap Index fund. Deploy ₹20,000 lumpsum into the aggressive hybrid fund. Set up SIPs — ₹4,800/month Large Cap, ₹2,400/month Midcap Index, ₹800/month Hybrid. Set up a Systematic Transfer Plan (STP) from the hybrid fund to the large cap fund over 6 months to reduce lumpsum concentration risk. Month 3: Review emergency fund goal — if emergency buffer of 3 months salary is not already in a bank savings account, redirect ₹10,000/month from SIP to savings until it is built. Once emergency fund is in place (assumed to be already achieved from frugal lifestyle), continue as plan.
review_checkpoints: Year 1 (2026): Confirm emergency fund is fully built (3 months salary). Confirm all SIPs are running on time. Do NOT change anything else.
Year 3 (2028): Reassess marriage goal timeline and corpus estimate. If marriage is still 3 years away, consider shifting ₹1L from equity to hybrid for stability around the marriage goal.
Year 6 (2031): Marriage goal is 1 year away. Shift 50% of marriage goal corpus into a safer fund (Aggressive Hybrid or short-duration debt fund). Do not touch equity corpus.
Year 10 (2035): First major review — increase SIPs based on salary growth. Reassess long-term horizon. Consider adding a Nifty Smallcap 250 index fund (passive only) as a satellite holding.
Year 20 (2045): 10 years to retirement. Start de-risking — shift 20-30% from equity to hybrid/debt. Increase hybrid allocation.
Year 25 (2050): 5 years to retirement. Shift 50% to hybrid and debt. Review tax implications of your corpus under new regime.
Year 30 (2055): Retirement. Withdraw only what you need incrementally. Consult a SEBI-registered investment advisor for withdrawal strategy.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Check your portfolio ONCE a year — ideally at the same time each year (e.g., February 1st or after salary increment). Rebalance only if any fund's allocation has drifted more than 5% from its target. For example, if your equity allocation (Large Cap + Midcap) drops below 85%, redirect new SIP money into equity funds until balance is restored. Do NOT sell funds to rebalance — this triggers taxes. Instead, adjust future SIP amounts to gradually correct drift. Never rebalance more than once a year. Never react to news, market crashes, or any short-term event.
plan.rationale: Katniss is 23, starting with a ₹50,000 corpus and ₹8,000/month in investible surplus. Her 30-year horizon makes equity the dominant player — equities have historically delivered ~14-15% CAGR over long cycles in India. The plan is built with three funds: (1) A large-cap index proxy (low-cost, passive), (2) a mid-cap index fund (growth tilt), and (3) a hybrid fund as the stability anchor. All three are equity-taxed under the new regime, meaning gains above ₹1.25L/year are taxed at just 12.5%. The hybrid fund is kept small (10%) due to its slab-taxation in new regime — it serves a portfolio stability role rather than a tax optimisation one. The SIP step-up is set at 10% annually to match salary growth. Total SIP: ₹8,000/month → grows to ~₹30,000/month by year 10. Corpus estimate at 30 years on 14.5% CAGR: ~₹1.2–1.5 crore with ₹8,000/month SIP + lumpsum.
risks: Market risk: Equity markets can fall 30-50% in bad years (2008, 2020). Don't stop SIPs during crashes — that's when you buy cheap.
Currency and foreign debt risk: Equity-taxed funds hold Indian stocks only — no currency or foreign debt exposure.
Inflation risk: Over 30 years, inflation can erode buying power. Equity is the best long-term inflation hedge.
Goal timing risk: Marriage goal is only 6 years away. If markets crash exactly then, you may fall short. Set aside the marriage corpus separately as it approaches.
Tax rule change risk: The new regime rules are 2024-specific. Future budget changes could alter tax treatment of hybrid funds. Review every year.
Behavioural risk: The biggest risk is doing nothing — not the market. Staying the course is harder than it sounds.
Hybrid fund tax note: The hybrid fund is equity-taxed only if it holds ≥65% stocks. If this changes in the future, your tax treatment may shift. Monitor fund composition annually.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase total SIP by 10% every year as salary grows. From ₹8,000/month today to ~₹30,000/month by Year 10. This alone makes a huge difference in final corpus.

allocation_schedule.trigger: Start of plan — 30-year horizon, moderate risk appetite, no gold allocation needed yet.
Year 3 review — marriage goal 3 years away, begin gradual de-risking.
Year 6 review — marriage goal 1 year away, add gold as inflation hedge.
Year 10 review — long-term wealth phase, maintain equity leadership.
Year 20 review — 10 years to retirement, begin meaningful de-risking.
Year 25 review — 5 years to retirement, shift to stability.

plan.perspective: Katniss, you're in the best position possible — young, starting with ₹50,000, ₹8,000/month to invest, and a 30-year runway. The single most powerful thing you can do is start now and never stop. This plan is simple: 60% in large Indian companies (index-style, low fee), 30% in mid-sized Indian companies (also index-style), and 10% in a hybrid fund that holds a mix of stocks and bonds for stability. Every rupee you invest today has about 30 years of compounding — that's the real wealth engine. And yes, you can start with just ₹8,000/month. The step-up of 10% every year is built in — it automatically matches your salary growth.

aps.reasoning: The plan demonstrates strong passive characteristics across multiple dimensions: 90% of equity allocation is passive (60% large-cap index proxy + 30% midcap index fund). Annual rebalancing with minimal intervention and explicit instructions against reactive trading support high turnover score. Cost minimization is emphasized with focus on low expense ratios (0.2% for midcap index). No individual security research - relies on broad market index exposure. 30-year horizon with infrequent reviews aligns perfectly with long-term passive investing. Portfolio metrics show index-like characteristics (beta ~1.0 for midcap, 0.88 for large cap) with moderate tracking error. The 10% hybrid fund allocation is actively managed but serves a specific stability purpose.

pqs.reasoning:
Goal Alignment (0.8): Plan aligns well with Katniss's long-term wealth building goal and 30-year horizon with 90% equity allocation. Addresses marriage goal timeline with specific de-risking steps. Emergency fund guidance is included but assumes it's already built.

Diversification (0.7): Good diversification across large cap (60%), mid cap (30%), and hybrid (10%) funds from different fund houses. However, lacks small cap exposure which would be appropriate for a 30-year horizon, and no international diversification.

Risk-Return Appropriateness (0.6): 90% equity allocation may be aggressive for 'moderate' risk appetite. Projected returns (13-16%) appear optimistic for primarily index funds. Hybrid fund allocation is too small to provide meaningful stability for a moderate investor.

Internal Consistency (0.9): Excellent consistency - rationale, allocations, rebalancing guidelines, and risk warnings all tell a coherent story. Clear timeline for goal-based adjustments and SIP step-up plan.

Tax Efficiency (0.3): Poor tax optimization for new_regime investor. Hybrid fund is slab-taxed for new regime investors but plan acknowledges this. Missing opportunity to use more equity-taxed alternatives. No mention of optimizing for new regime tax structure beyond basic fund selection.
